selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

messages.content: You must return JSON only. No markdown, no code fences, no commentary. Return a single JSON object with keys: mode, topic, opinion, reasoning_summary.

Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a 52-year-old plumber in Los Angeles, I don't use social media myself, but I see its impact through my three kids and volunteer work. Managing fake content starts with good old-fashioned skepticism and common sense, much like checking a pipe for leaks before assuming it's fine. I'm naturally curious, so I encourage people to dig deeper—don't just accept what pops up on a screen. In my job, I rely on vocational training and hands-on experience, and similarly, folks should verify information through trusted sources, like local news or community leaders, rather than sharing posts impulsively. My Christian beliefs teach honesty and integrity, so I think spreading fake content harms our social fabric, and we all have a duty to stop it. With high conscientiousness and agreeableness, I believe in taking responsibility: if you encounter fake news, report it calmly and educate others gently, maybe during a yoga class or while gardening with neighbors. Since I'm single and busy with work and hobbies like reading novels and rock climbing, I value real-world connections over online noise; fostering face-to-face discussions can help combat misinformation. Ultimately, dealing with fake content isn't about fancy tech—it's about using your head, heart, and community ties to build a more truthful world.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion from persona traits: age (52) suggests life experience; occupation (plumber) implies practical, hands-on approach; no social media use leads to outsider perspective emphasizing real-world solutions; psychological feature (curious) supports investigative stance; religious beliefs (Christianity) inform ethical duty against dishonesty; OCEAN traits (high conscientiousness, high agreeableness) promote responsibility and gentle education; hobbies (volunteering, gardening) indicate community focus; children (3) and city (Los Angeles) provide context for social impact. Avoided technical jargon, aligned tone with moderate openness and low neuroticism for balanced, calm prose.
